title: Cat Excessive Licking of Genital Area: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why cats excessively lick their genital area, the urgency of the situation, and how to identify potential urinary or skin issues.
content_html:
TL;DR: Excessive genital licking in cats often indicates underlying pain or inflammation and requires a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours, but an inability to urinate is a life-threatening emergency.

What does it mean if my cat is excessively licking their genitals?

When a cat focuses obsessively on grooming their genital or urinary region, it is often a sign of underlying discomfort rather than a simple hygiene routine. This behavior usually indicates inflammation, irritation, or pain in the urinary tract or the surrounding skin. You may notice redness, fur loss, or even small sores in the area due to the abrasive nature of a cat's tongue.

Is excessive genital licking in cats a medical emergency?

  • Medium Urgency: Most cases require a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours to prevent the condition from worsening.
  • Critical Emergency: If your cat is straining to urinate, vocalizing in pain, or unable to produce any urine, seek immediate emergency care as this may indicate a life-threatening urethral obstruction.

How does taking a photo of my cat’s genital area help with veterinary triage?

  • Allows a professional to see visible swelling, abnormal discharge, or skin trauma caused by licking.
  • Helps determine whether the issue is a surface-level skin infection or an internal problem.
  • Provides visual data to decide if urgent diagnostic tests, such as a urinalysis, are necessary.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive licking of the genital area in cats may be due to underlying medical problems that cause pruritus or pain. Potential causes include disorders of micturition such as urethral incompetence, anatomic abnormalities (hooded vulva, patent urachus, ectopic ureters, uroliths), urinary tract infection, and concurrent diseases (chronic kidney disease, hyperadrenocorticism, diabetes mellitus). Diagnostic considerations should include abdominal radiographs, ultrasonography, cystoscopy, and/or double-contrast cystourethrography to exclude radiolucent urocystoliths, anatomic defects, polyps, and neoplasia. A serum biochemical profile, CBC, and complete urinalysis are important to exclude predisposing systemic diseases, as well as testing for feline immunodeficiency virus, feline leukemia virus, and hyperthyroidism.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine, Dermatology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Could my cat have a urinary tract infection (UTI)?
faqs.answer: Yes, UTIs or Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) are common causes of genital licking because the inflammation makes the area feel uncomfortable or itchy.

faqs.question: Can allergies cause this behavior?
faqs.answer: Yes, environmental or food allergies can lead to localized skin irritation, prompting the cat to lick the area for relief.

faqs.question: What should I watch for at the litter box?
faqs.answer: Watch for frequent trips to the box, blood in the urine, or crying while attempting to go, as these are signs of a more serious medical issue.
